title: Binary Shuffle

content.problemStatement:
Chef has two integers AAA and BBB. He can perform the following operation on AAA an arbitrary number of times (including zero):


write AAA as a binary number with an arbitrary number of leading zeroes (possibly without any)
shuffle the binary digits of AAA in an arbitrary way, obtaining a number sss
replace AAA by s+1s+1s+1
Chef is wondering about the minimum number of operations he has to perform on AAA in order to obtain BBB. Compute this number or determine that it is impossible.
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains two space‑separated integers A and B (0 ≤ A, B ≤ 10^18).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the minimum number of operations required to transform A into B, or -1 if it is impossible.

solution.approach: For a single operation we can permute the bits of A arbitrarily (including adding leading zeros) and then add 1. The permutation can change only the number of set bits, not their count. After shuffling, the resulting number s has the same popcount as A. Adding 1 may change the popcount: popcount(s+1)=popcount(s)-k+1 where k is the number of trailing ones of s. By choosing a suitable permutation we can make k any value from 0 up to popcount(s). Consequently, after one operation we can obtain any number whose popcount equals popcount(A)-k+1 for some k∈[0,popcount(A)]. Repeating the operation, the popcount can only increase by at most 1 each step. Therefore the only reachable numbers are those B such that popcount(B‑1) ≥ popcount(A). If this condition holds, the minimum number of operations is (popcount(B‑1)‑popcount(A)+1). Special cases: A==B → 0; B==0 → impossible unless A==0 (handled by the formula); B==1 → possible only from A==0 in one operation. The algorithm computes popcounts in O(1) using built‑in functions and applies the formula.
solution.timeComplexity: O(1) per test case, O(T) total.
